This is currently implemented using the approx_top_k function in Snowflake. It refactors the pretty printed return and does an exact calculation on which items pass PySpark's support parameter threshold. PySpark uses an approximation so there are some false positives in their result.

D2 API - In Spark, each row from the original DataFrame is assigned to exactly one of the output DataFrames (though this behavior is not documented). In SAS, due to Snowflake's sampling implementation limitations, rows may appear in multiple output DataFrames or be excluded entirely.\n * The `seed` argument is ignored due to restrictions in Snowflake's sample implementation. Snowflake does not allow seed argument for sample queries that are on views, and due to the nested nature of queries we generate, all sample queries end up being queries on views.

PySpark converts decimal literals to integers using ceiling in comparisons (e.g., b < 2.5 becomes b < 3), while SAS preserves exact decimal values (keeps b < 2.5 as-is). We shouldn't fix it because SAS's behavior is mathematically correct and more intuitive - users expect 2.5 to remain 2.5, not be secretly converted to 3, which can produce incorrect results in edge cases.
